name: Under Armour, Inc.

profile.description: Under Armour, Inc. is a sportswear, footwear, and accessories company headquartered in Baltimore, Maryland. Founded in 1996 by Kevin Plank, the company is undergoing a restructuring plan costing up to $255 million after revenue declined to approximately $5.0 billion in fiscal 2026 (ending March 2026). The share price has declined 85% from its 2015 record high.

innovation.label: WEAK
innovation.analysis: Innovation Track Record (0/3): Under Armour has failed to innovate meaningfully in the last five years, missing the sneakers and athleisure trends and 'essentially handing the market to Lululemon while strengthening Nike' [2]. The $1B spent on fitness app acquisitions (2014-2016) was later divested. Revenue has declined from $5.7B to $5.0B. AI Deployment (0/2): No evidence of AI product deployment or measurable AI-driven operational improvements in apparel design, supply chain, or retail [2]. Market Share Leadership (1/2): Under Armour maintains a recognizable brand with $5B revenue but has lost significant market share; market cap of $2.6B vs. Lululemon ($59B) and Nike ($153B) reflects competitive decline [2]. Product Pipeline (1/2): The Curry Brand separation and restructuring plan signal new direction, with FY2026 adjusted operating income raised to $95-110M [1]. R&D Intensity (0/1): No evidence of above-average R&D investment; innovation has been execution-poor.

inspiration.label: WEAK
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (1/4): Under Armour's mission to 'make you better through passion, design, and the relentless pursuit of innovation' is generic and unvalidated by recent execution [2]. Community Impact (0/2): No distinctive charitable or community programs were identified in the research. Employee Retention (0/2): Strip club culture revelations, three CEO changes in five years (Plank, Frisk, Linnartz, Plank again), and Plank's defense of micromanagement signal a troubled work environment [2]. Glassdoor and analyst commentary describe a 'very difficult company to lead' with 'checkered resume for driving cultural excellence' [2]. Insider Buying (1/2): Plank returned as CEO despite criticism, suggesting personal commitment to the brand he founded, but his return was widely criticized by analysts and Wall Street [2].

infrastructure.label: WEAK
infrastructure.analysis: Under Armour's infrastructure is weak: negative profit margin of -10.4%, negative ROE of -30.4%, D/E of 117.7%, and forward P/E of 26.5 [1]. Revenue declined ~4% in FY2026 with North America down 8% and APAC down 14%. The restructuring plan costs up to $255M. However, adjusted operating income guidance was raised to $95-110M, and EMEA grew 12% [1]. The company's $2.6B market cap is 85% below its 2015 peak. Liquidity is adequate at $74.2M daily dollar volume.

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (7/9): Under Armour's core business — designing, manufacturing, and distributing physical sportswear and footwear — involves physical products, supply chains, and retail distribution that AI cannot directly displace [1]. The brand, athlete endorsements, and physical retail presence create barriers to AI substitution. However, AI could disrupt design workflows and customer acquisition. AI Opportunity (0/1): No evidence of AI revenue generation or meaningful AI product deployment; the company lags far behind competitors in digital and AI capabilities [2].

infinite_game.summary: Under Armour is a deeply finite company under founder Kevin Plank's return. The revolving door of CEOs (three in five years), strip club culture revelations, micromanagement defense, and focus on restructuring costs and quarterly earnings all point to a company playing to survive this quarter rather than build something enduring. Plank's inability to build lasting leadership beyond himself is the defining finite trait.

just_cause.analysis: No Just Cause evident (0/3). Under Armour's mission of 'relentless pursuit of innovation' rings hollow after years of failed innovation and market share losses to Lululemon and Nike [2]. The company has no articulated vision of a better future — it exists to sell athletic apparel, and hasn't even done that well recently.

trusting_teams.analysis: Low-trust environment (0/2). Three CEO changes in five years (Plank out 2020, Frisk ousted 2022, Linnartz ousted 2024, Plank returns 2024), strip club culture revelations, and Plank's public defense of micromanagement create a deeply unstable organizational environment [2]. Analysts describe Under Armour as 'a very difficult company to lead' with culture that 'retains much of the DNA from its founding.'

worthy_rivals.analysis: Finite competitive mindset (0/1). Plank has historically focused on 'beating' Nike and gaining market share rather than learning from competitors' strengths [2]. The company missed the athleisure trend (Lululemon) and sneaker culture (Nike) because leadership was focused on its own vision rather than learning from market dynamics.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Some flexibility (1/2). The separation of the Curry Brand and the $255M restructuring plan show willingness to change direction [1]. However, these are defensive restructuring moves rather than visionary pivots — the core business model of athletic apparel and footwear remains unchanged, just smaller.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Pattern of courage (2/2). Despite universal Wall Street criticism, Plank returned as CEO to lead the turnaround himself [2]. Raising adjusted operating income guidance to $95-110M while revenue declines shows willingness to make painful restructuring decisions. The Curry Brand separation and acceptance of $255M in restructuring charges demonstrate willingness to sacrifice near-term results for long-term health [1].

investment_thesis: Under Armour offers a deep-value turnaround play at $6.04/share (85% below 2015 highs), with founder Kevin Plank returning as CEO to execute a $255M restructuring targeting $95-110M adjusted operating income in FY2026 [1]. EMEA revenue grew 12%, demonstrating that the brand resonates internationally even as North America struggles. The Curry Brand separation creates a cleaner narrative. At 26.5x forward P/E, the stock is pricing in a modest recovery, and any reacceleration in North America could generate significant upside from a depressed base. The company expects fiscal 2027 stabilization in North America and APAC [1].
thesis_continued: The bear case is compelling: revenue has declined for multiple consecutive years to ~$5.0B, North America down 8%, and APAC down 14% [1]. Founder Plank's return was universally criticized by Wall Street, and his leadership is associated with cultural problems including strip club practices, micromanagement, and the inability to retain talented CEOs [2]. The brand has lost relevance versus Lululemon and Nike, with no clear innovation pipeline. Profit margin is -10.4%, ROE is -30.4%, and the $255M restructuring charges will depress near-term earnings further. The 2.7% revenue growth forecast sits far below the 10.2% U.S. market benchmark, and recovery depends entirely on Plank executing a turnaround he's been unable to achieve in prior attempts [1][2].
key_catalysts: North America and APAC stabilization in fiscal 2027 following the completion of the $255M restructuring plan [1]
Adjusted operating income guidance raised to $95-110M, demonstrating margin improvement from restructuring [1]
EMEA revenue growth of 12% in Q2 FY2026 could expand if the international momentum continues [1]
key_risks: Founder-CEO Plank's return may fail to reverse brand decline; prior CEOs could not fix the company's problems, and Plank may not either [2]
North America revenue declining 8% with no clear catalyst for reversal; Lululemon and Nike continue to strengthen competitive positions [1]
Cultural dysfunction including strip club revelations, micromanagement, and serial CEO turnover creates ongoing talent retention risk [2]
sources: Under Armour Q3 FY2026 Results and Updated FY2026 Outlook, February 2026
Yahoo Finance, Fortune, Fast Company, CNBC — Kevin Plank leadership analysis and cultural criticism, 2024-2026
